These aren’t our ideas, they’re what people said we should do.

- Who’s "people"? Chatham House rules, but I can say they’re from places like OpenAI, Anthropic, DeepMind, Mila, AMD, the EU AI Office, Scale AI, GovAI, etc.

- Who’s "we"? You: anyone who wants to help.

text:
At the start of the year, the Peregrine crew interviewed 48 experts asking for ideas. 

The framing was about what you can do *fast*, if we only had ~3 years. So interpret them in that light, not as unconditional recommendations. 

Some of our favourites: https://t.co/OsuKDQiYk0

text:
In expectation nothing ever happens (Martingale)
Up to small perturbations nothing ever happens (KAM)
Eventually it's as if nothing happened (Poincaré recurrence)
Once nothing happens it tends to keep happening (Le Chatelier)
The arc of the universe tends towards nothing happening (H-theorem)

text:
New paper on a long-shot I've been obsessed with for a year:

How much are AI reasoning gains confounded by expanding the training corpus 10000x? How much LLM performance is down to "local" generalisation (pattern-matching to hard-to-detect semantically equivalent training data)? https://t.co/utpmbXIK93

raw.full_text:
New paper on a long-shot I've been obsessed with for a year:

How much are AI reasoning gains confounded by expanding the training corpus 10000x? How much LLM performance is down to "local" generalisation (pattern-matching to hard-to-detect semantically equivalent training data)? https://t.co/utpmbXIK93

text:
tl;dr 

* OLMo 3 training corpus contains exact duplicates of 50% of the ZebraLogic test set.

* We embed the corpus to find semantic duplicates of test data in the wild. 78% of the CodeForces test set had >=1 semantic duplicate

* The semantic duplicate rate is maybe >4 in 10000

raw.full_text:
tl;dr 

* OLMo 3 training corpus contains exact duplicates of 50% of the ZebraLogic test set.

* We embed the corpus to find semantic duplicates of test data in the wild. 78% of the CodeForces test set had >=1 semantic duplicate

* The semantic duplicate rate is maybe >4 in 10000

text: https://t.co/CH2vP0Y7OF

raw.full_text: https://t.co/CH2vP0Y7OF

text:
Imagine you're head of training at at OpenAI, and you want your benchmark scores to be meaningful (: to estimate OOD performance)

You have a hard task ahead of you! Your models have seen so much, memorisation is so easy - as is *local generalisation* (noisy pattern-matching).

raw.full_text:
Imagine you're head of training at at OpenAI, and you want your benchmark scores to be meaningful (: to estimate OOD performance)

You have a hard task ahead of you! Your models have seen so much, memorisation is so easy - as is *local generalisation* (noisy pattern-matching).

text: What can you do? Well, obviously you take every benchmark you're going to test on and try to "decontaminate" your training corpus (remove test data from the training data).

raw.full_text: What can you do? Well, obviously you take every benchmark you're going to test on and try to "decontaminate" your training corpus (remove test data from the training data).

text:
By default this is just one level above string matching ("n-gram matching" - if sentences overlap in (say) a 13-token window, remove them from the training corpus).

But you're actually trying, so you also translate the test sets and delete translations of test from train.

raw.full_text:
By default this is just one level above string matching ("n-gram matching" - if sentences overlap in (say) a 13-token window, remove them from the training corpus).

But you're actually trying, so you also translate the test sets and delete translations of test from train.

text: But! every piece of test data has an arbitrary number of logical equivalents and neighbours (like how `x + y = 10` is the same problem as `2x + 2y = 20`). And LLMs are amazing at semantic search, so maybe this inflates benchmark scores.

raw.full_text: But! every piece of test data has an arbitrary number of logical equivalents and neighbours (like how `x + y = 10` is the same problem as `2x + 2y = 20`). And LLMs are amazing at semantic search, so maybe this inflates benchmark scores.

text: The cutting-edge tech for detecting these "semantic" duplicates is... an LLM. But you simply can't do 100T x 1M calls. There's not enough compute in the world (yet).

raw.full_text: The cutting-edge tech for detecting these "semantic" duplicates is... an LLM. But you simply can't do 100T x 1M calls. There's not enough compute in the world (yet).

text:
So you do what you can - maybe you 

* categorise the entire corpus & do intense search inside relevant partitions (e.g. maths > number theory > ...)
* embed the whole corpus & look for things really close to test data 
* train a wee 300M filter model & do what you can with that

raw.full_text:
So you do what you can - maybe you 

* categorise the entire corpus & do intense search inside relevant partitions (e.g. maths > number theory > ...)
* embed the whole corpus & look for things really close to test data 
* train a wee 300M filter model & do what you can with that

text:
How much does this process catch? How many semantic duplicates of test data slip through? And what's the impact on final benchmark scores?

We don't know, This (finally) is where our paper comes in:

raw.full_text:
How much does this process catch? How many semantic duplicates of test data slip through? And what's the impact on final benchmark scores?

We don't know, This (finally) is where our paper comes in:

text:
We experiment on OLMo 3, one of the only really good models with open training data. 

Since we have its entire training corpus, we can exhaustively check for real "natural" duplicates and finetune it to estimate their impact. We embed the entire Dolma Instruct corpus.

raw.full_text:
We experiment on OLMo 3, one of the only really good models with open training data. 

Since we have its entire training corpus, we can exhaustively check for real "natural" duplicates and finetune it to estimate their impact. We embed the entire Dolma Instruct corpus.

text: Firstly: we were surprised by how ineffective n-gram decontamination was at catching exact duplicates - 70% of harder tasks had a match. But the spurious performance gain wasn't so large, at most +4pp. https://t.co/9ntlkm1ZbN

raw.full_text: Firstly: we were surprised by how ineffective n-gram decontamination was at catching exact duplicates - 70% of harder tasks had a match. But the spurious performance gain wasn't so large, at most +4pp. https://t.co/9ntlkm1ZbN

text:
Thirdly we generated 10k synthetic duplicates for MuSR, Zebralogic, and MBPP problems and finetuned on them. 

* MuSR +22pp. Semantic duplicates as strong as exact
* ZebraLogic +12pp. Exact much stronger
* MBPP +17pp. Exact stronger https://t.co/LKyWwr9Z94

raw.full_text:
Thirdly we generated 10k synthetic duplicates for MuSR, Zebralogic, and MBPP problems and finetuned on them. 

* MuSR +22pp. Semantic duplicates as strong as exact
* ZebraLogic +12pp. Exact much stronger
* MBPP +17pp. Exact stronger https://t.co/LKyWwr9Z94

text: Secondly, every single MBPP test example and 78% of CodeForces have semantic duplicates https://t.co/Z0pV2J9Xo5

raw.full_text: Secondly, every single MBPP test example and 78% of CodeForces have semantic duplicates https://t.co/Z0pV2J9Xo5

text: Fourthly we guess that 4 in 10,000 training datapoints are a strong semantic duplicate for a given benchmark datapoint (where strong means just "obvious to Gemini")

raw.full_text: Fourthly we guess that 4 in 10,000 training datapoints are a strong semantic duplicate for a given benchmark datapoint (where strong means just "obvious to Gemini")

text:
So: 

n-gram decontamination is not enough even for the easy (exact) stuff, semantic duplicates are at least a moderately big deal, and this probably transfers to frontier models to some degree.

The above are probably underestimates too (since our detection pipeline was cheapo).

text:

And local generalisation across very different strings is anyway pretty miraculous https://t.co/Z7WpuhmCQc

raw.full_text: We have a lot of ideas! If you're interested in funding this, grab me at gavin@arbresearch.com

text:
The grand aim of this research programme is to decompose benchmark gains / apparent AI progress into 4 estimates:

1. benchmaxxing (memorising exact duplicates)
2. usemaxxing (RLing narrow capabilities)
3. hidden interpolation / local generalisation
4. OOD generalisation

text:
@LeRoyDesCimes 2. why does this text give us evidence about the actual reasoning process used in the activations?

Happy accident. It learned how to use its own context as working notes, and we rewarded it such that the text tends to be a causally-relevant summary of the latent computation

text:
@joodalooped Some useful words: 

* Counterfactual-evaluation gap: they do way worse on stuff they haven't seen. Model task perf is indeed strongly dependent on task training data.

@joodalooped Some useful words:

text:
@joodalooped * Synthetic reach: But a lot of this data is nonhuman now. So it's often not parroting humans but instead hard data or a computer environment

* Model laziness: they memorise when they can rather than thinking

raw.full_text:
@joodalooped * Synthetic reach: But a lot of this data is nonhuman now. So it's often not parroting humans but instead hard data or a computer environment

* Model laziness: they memorise when they can rather than thinking

text:
@joodalooped * Shallow generalisation: they go beyond literal memorisation all the time, are very impressive at noting and exploiting similarities to things they've seen

* Bag of heuristics: they contain many exact good algorithms but they sometimes struggle to compose them correctly

text:
Open question: should you aim to codify your morals in an institution?

Pros:
+ get to impose it on everyone
+ vast state-level resources

Cons
- Co-option, goodharting
- "Pessimisation" (the bureau implementation can harm the cause even if you win on the politics)
- Backlash

text: @natsothanaphan I appreciate your kind warning, but I disagree. Validation of results is of course a hard problem (even when base rates of slop were ~2% rather than the 90% we're inviting), and yes even autoformalisation is vulnerable to adversarial pressure and misdefinition -

raw.full_text: @natsothanaphan I appreciate your kind warning, but I disagree. Validation of results is of course a hard problem (even when base rates of slop were ~2% rather than the 90% we're inviting), and yes even autoformalisation is vulnerable to adversarial pressure and misdefinition -

text: @natsothanaphan But! we get some control over the false positive : false negative tradeoff, & can set this so our human reviewers aren't overwhelmed. Not everyone needs to follow the old journal compact of maximum sensitivity and K-selection.

raw.full_text: @natsothanaphan But! we get some control over the false positive : false negative tradeoff, & can set this so our human reviewers aren't overwhelmed. Not everyone needs to follow the old journal compact of maximum sensitivity and K-selection.

text: @natsothanaphan Failure is very possible (most obviously, we might be too early for these tools to support the weight we're putting on them; it might be that the community rejects degrees of validation and needs the old 99.8% confidence to do anything at all with results) but it's worth a go imo
